# Technical Details {.appendix}

The goal is to narrow the funnel so lawyers only review clauses that are likely real. Every stage cuts down the number of candidates.

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-cap: "Each stage narrows the funnel. Lawyers only see the most likely matches."
flowchart TD
    A["4,769 documents from EDGAR, NSM, PDIP"] --> B["Parse with Docling"]
    B --> C["Locate candidates with deterministic pattern matching"]
    C --> D["~15,000 candidate sections"]
    D --> E["Extract and filter with Claude Opus + Sonnet"]
    E --> F["9,145 likely clause matches"]
    F --> G["Lawyer review (the missing piece)"]
```

**Parse.** Documents are parsed with [Docling](https://github.com/DS4SD/docling), which extracts text with document structure (headings, sections, tables) preserved.

**Locate.** Deterministic regex pattern matching finds candidate sections. Patterns are organized by clause family (heading cues, body-text cues). A section needs cue hits from multiple pattern families to qualify. This is fast, interpretable, and catches the obvious candidates.

**Extract.** Claude Opus and Sonnet receive each candidate section with multi-shot prompts showing examples from PDIP's expert-annotated contracts. The models extract the clause verbatim and flag low-confidence matches. This step cuts out the false positives that pattern matching alone cannot catch: table of contents entries, summaries, cross-references.

**Verify.** Every extraction is checked against the source text using fuzzy string matching (95% similarity threshold). This catches paraphrasing, hallucination, and OCR drift.

**What we can measure.** Recall against PDIP expert annotations (did we find what experts tagged?). Verbatim similarity to source text. What we cannot measure without lawyer review: precision (false positive rate) and inter-rater reliability.

**The missing piece is human validation.** The pipeline narrows thousands of documents down to a manageable set of likely matches. But "likely" is not "correct." Only a lawyer can confirm that.
